title: Real-Time DeepLab Tradeoffs
concept: Deploying DeepLab in real-world systems requires balancing the computational cost of ASPP against accuracy by choosing light backbones.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main structural improvement in DeepLab v3+?

options.text: Adding a decoder module to refine upsampled boundaries
Removing all dilated convolutions to save speed
Replacing the encoder with a simple dense linear layer

exercises.explain: Correct! DeepLab v3+ uses an encoder-decoder architecture.

cards.title: The resource hurdle
cards.body: DeepLab v3+ is incredibly accurate, winning many vision competitions. But its accuracy comes from a heavy backbone (like ResNet-101) and complex ASPP convolutions. If you run this on a mobile phone or a robot, the battery will drain in minutes. How do we tune DeepLab for real-time systems?

cards.title: DeepLab Tradeoffs
cards.body: To use DeepLab in real-time systems, developers trade off backbone depth. Swapping a heavy ResNet backbone for MobileNetv2 or MobileNetv3 reduces parameters by up to 90%, enabling execution on edge hardware with minimal accuracy loss.

cards.title: Putting a small engine in a truck
cards.body: Running DeepLab with ResNet-101 is like putting a massive V8 engine in a truck — powerful but uses lots of fuel (energy). Swapping in MobileNet is like installing a hybrid engine: it gets you where you need to go while saving gas.

cards.title: Mobile DeepLab
cards.body: Google's portrait mode on Pixel phones uses a MobileNet-backed DeepLab model. It runs in milliseconds to blur your photo background without making your phone hot.

cards.title: ASPP is not free
cards.body: You might think ASPP is fast because it uses dilated filters. However, running five parallel convolution paths (as in ASPP) requires significant memory bandwidth, which can bottleneck mobile processors.

left.text:
Best for: Servers, medical analysis
Highest accuracy
Slow (10 FPS on edge)

right.text:
Best for: Smartphones, drones
Good accuracy
Real-time (30+ FPS on edge)

exercises.prompt: Which backbone is best for deploying DeepLab on a mobile device?
exercises.explain: Correct! MobileNet is lightweight and optimized for mobile processors.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The ASPP module has low memory bandwidth requirements due to dilation.
exercises.explain: False. Running parallel conv paths in ASPP is memory-heavy and can bottleneck mobile chips.

exercises.sentence: Swapping a heavy backbone for MobileNet reduces the number of model ___ by up to 90%.
exercises.explain: It decreases parameters and compute, enabling edge deployment.

exercises.prompt: What real-world smartphone feature often uses optimized DeepLab models?

options.text: Predicting when the battery will die

exercises.explain: Portrait mode requires fast, precise segmentation of the subject.

exercises.prompt: You are building a live camera filter app for old smartphones. The filter needs to segment hair to color it. Which setup should you choose?

options.text: DeepLab with a MobileNet backbone, to ensure the model runs in real-time without lagging the phone.
DeepLab with a ResNet-101 backbone, to get the absolute highest resolution.
A standard classification network, because hair does not need a mask.
DeepLab v1 with a CRF post-processor, to get sharp edges.

exercises.explain: Perfect! MobileNet-backed DeepLab is the industry standard for mobile real-time segmentation. Next, let's explore loss functions! Phase 9: Image Segmentation is complete. Next up: Advanced Vision Applications.
